coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on dual-emission carbon dots for brilliant blue detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Chen, Meng

Liu, Zhixiong

Wang, Ligang
Feng, Feng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform based on blue-yellow dual-emission carbon dots (BY-CDs) was constructed for the first time to determine brilliant blue. The BY-CDs was synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal process involving propyl gallate and o-phenylenediamine. The synthesized BY-CDs exhibit favorable water solubility and exceptional fluorescence stability. Under excitation at 370 nm, BY-CDs show two distinguishable fluorescence emission bands (458 and 558 nm). Upon addition of brilliant blue, the fluorescence intensity at 558 nm exhibited a significant quenching effect attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the fluorescence intensity at 458 nm was basically unchanged. The prepared BY-CDs can effectively serve as a ratiometric nanosensor for determining brilliant blue with the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 458 and 558 nm (F458/F558) as response signal. In addition, the developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibits a noticeable alteration in color from yellow to green under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm upon addition of varying concentrations of brilliant blue, which provides the possibility of visual detection of brilliant blue by a smartphone application. Finally, the BY-CDs based dual-mode sensing platform successfully detected brilliant blue in actual food samples and achieved a desirable recovery rate. This study highlights the merits of fast, convenient, economical, real-time, visual, high accuracy, excellent precision, good selectivity and high sensitivity for brilliant blue detection, and paves new paths for the monitoring of brilliant blue in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Smartphone-assisted sensing platform
Dual-emission carbon dots
Brilliant blue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of brilliant blue
Smartphone-assisted visual detection of brilliant blue
Optimization and ratiometric fluorescence detection of brilliant blue
Selectivity and anti-interference of BY-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor
The detection performance of BY-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor in real sample
BY-CDs based smartphone-assisted sensor for brilliant blue detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The XPS full-scan spectrum, (B) C1s high resolution spectrum, (C) N1s high resolution spectrum and (D) O1s high resolution spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of BY-CDs solution. Inset: photograph of the BY-CDs solution under natural light (left) and UV lamp (right). (B) Fluorescence spectra of BY-CDs solution at different excitation wavelengths (350–440 nm).
(A) The fluorescence spectrum of BY-CDs after adding different concentrations of brilliant blue and (B) the linear relationship between F458/F558 and concentrations of brilliant blue.
(A) F458/F558 of BY-CDs in the presence of brilliant blue and other different interferents. (B) F458/F558 of BY-CDs and brilliant blue system in the presence of different interferents.
(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra of brilliant blue and the excitation and emission spectra of BY-CDs. (B) The fluorescence lifetime of BY-CDs before and after adding brilliant blue.

(A) The linear relationship between G/R and the concentrations of brilliant blue. Inset: the fluorescent image of the sensing system after adding different concentrations of brilliant blue under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light. (B) CIE chromaticity diagram of the sensor in the presence of different concentrations of brilliant blue.
A ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform based on BY-CDs was designed to detect brilliant blue.
Detection of brilliant blue in real samples using ratiometric fluorescence sensor (n = 6).

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted sensing platform based on dual-emission carbon dots for brilliant blue detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030032, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030032, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, PR China (F. Feng).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform based on blue-yellow dual-emission carbon dots (BY-CDs) was designed to detect brilliant blue.

In this study, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensing platform based on blue-yellow dual-emission carbon dots (BY-CDs) was constructed for the first time to determine brilliant blue. The BY-CDs was synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal process involving propyl gallate and o-phenylenediamine. The synthesized BY-CDs exhibit favorable water solubility and exceptional fluorescence stability. Under excitation at 370 nm, BY-CDs show two distinguishable fluorescence emission bands (458 and 558 nm). Upon addition of brilliant blue, the fluorescence intensity at 558 nm exhibited a significant quenching effect attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the fluorescence intensity at 458 nm was basically unchanged. The prepared BY-CDs can effectively serve as a ratiometric nanosensor for determining brilliant blue with the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 458 and 558 nm (F458/F558) as response signal. In addition, the developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor exhibits a noticeable alteration in color from yellow to green under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm upon addition of varying concentrations of brilliant blue, which provides the possibility of visual detection of brilliant blue by a smartphone application. Finally, the BY-CDs based dual-mode sensing platform successfully detected brilliant blue in actual food samples and achieved a desirable recovery rate. This study highlights the merits of fast, convenient, economical, real-time, visual, high accuracy, excellent precision, good selectivity and high sensitivity for brilliant blue detection, and paves new paths for the monitoring of brilliant blue in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Brilliant blue is an artificially synthesized food colorant that can improve the appearance and color of foods. Compared with natural colorants, it has lower cost, longer color retention time, better oxidation stability and thermal stability, and is commonly used in foods such as candies, beverages, pastries and jellies [1]. The national standard GB 2760-2014 (Standard for Use of Food Additives) clearly stipulates that the maximum allowable addition amount of brilliant blue in food is 500 mg/Kg. Although brilliant blue is allowed as a food colorant, excessive addition can affect the quality and safety of foods. Related reports have indicated that excessive intake of brilliant blue can cause urticaria, cancer, asthma, allergic reactions, insomnia and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder in children [2,3]. Thus, it is imperative to establish a convenient, accurate and sensitive method for monitoring brilliant blue to guarantee the well-being and security of individuals. At present, several analytical methods, such as capillary electrophoresis [4], UV spectrophotometry [5], electrochemical sensor [6,7], surface-enhanced raman scattering [8] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9] have been used to detect brilliant blue. Although these methods have made significant contributions to the determination of brilliant blue, they have drawbacks such as complex sample pretreatment, high cost, requiring skilled operators and long-time consumption, which restrict their practical applicability. Thus, it is imperative for researchers to make great efforts to develop a straightforward and highly sensitive method for detecting brilliant blue. Fluorescence method is considered a promising analytical tool because of its simplicity in operation, economic, exceptional sensitivity and fast response time [10].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have garnered significant attention owing to its remarkable water solubility, exceptional luminescence, outstanding biocompatibility, high stability and convenient preparation [11–13]. It has been extensively employed in the field of biological imaging [14,15], drug delivery [16], sensor [17,18], electrocatalysis [19] and energy conversion [20]. In particular, the application of CDs as fluorescence sensor has been implemented for detecting diverse substances [15,21–23]. However, the response of most fluorescence sensors based on CDs depends on a single-emission and is susceptible to interference from external factors such as background signals, fluorescent probes’ local concentration, system’s microenvironment, fluctuation in the light source and the complexity of actual samples [24,25]. The dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor can effectively eliminate or reduce interference by evaluating the intensity ratio between two emission peaks [26,27]. It has strong self-calibration ability and greatly improves the accuracy of detection. In order to construct a dual-emission sensing system, CDs are usually coupled with other fluorescent materials through chemical bonds or physical forces. These fluorescent materials include metal organic frameworks [28,29], metal nanoclusters [30,31], organic dyes [32], lanthanide ions [33], etc. Since the dual-emission sensing system consists of two or more fluorescent materials, it has the shortcomings of high cost and complex operation [34]. In recent years, dual-emission CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensors have received great favor from researchers due to their straightforward fabrication process and low cost.

In this study, an innovative sensing platform based on blue-yellow dual-emission CDs (BY-CDs) was developed for the first time to detect brilliant blue, incorporating ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensor (Scheme 1 ). The BY-CDs was synthesized via a straightforward hydrothermal method utilizing propyl gallate and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials. The BY-CDs show two independent emission bands at 458 and 558 nm when excited at a wavelength of 370 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the BY-CDs at 558 nm was found to be significantly quenched by brilliant blue via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), while the intensity of emission peak at 458 nm kept unchanged. Based on this, the successful construction of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for detecting brilliant blue. Moreover, on the basis of the above sensing system, a smartphone-assisted visual sensor was developed to detect brilliant blue by analyzing RGB (Red, green and blue) values. The proposed dual-mode sensing platform offers several advantages, including rapid response time, cost-effectiveness, simplicity of operation, real-time monitoring capability, visual output, high sensitivity, robust stability and excellent selectivity. Finally, it was employed for the analysis of brilliant blue in candy and beverage samples with highly desirable outcomes.

Propyl gallate, o-phenylenediamine, sodium benzoate, aspartame, acesulfame, vitamin C and vitamin B1 were provided by Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd (Shantou, China). Brilliant blue, glucose, maltose, fructose and sucrose were provided by Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ferric chloride (FeCl3), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl), cupric chloride (CuCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan and leucine were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were prepared by combining a 10 mM NaH2PO4 solution with a 10 mM Na2HPO4 solution and adjusting the different pH values using either 1 M NaOH or 1 M HCl. All the reagents mentioned above were of high purity grade and are ready for immediate use without requiring any purification treatment. The experiment was conducted using ultra-purified water supplied by a Millipore Milli-Q system (USA) throughout the entire duration.

BY-CDs were prepared as follows: o-phenylenediamine (5.4 mg) and propyl gallate (10.6 mg) were introduced to a solution of 10 mL ultra-purified water, followed by subjecting the mixture to ultrasound treatment for a duration of 20 min. The homogeneous mixture was moved into a teflon-coated autoclave and underwent a reaction at 180 ℃ for a duration of 4 h within a drying oven. After the completion of the reaction, the BY-CDs crude product was subjected to filtration (0.22 μm), followed by dialysis with a Spectrum Laboratories cellulose ester dialysis bag (MWCO: 500 Da) and freeze-drying to obtain powders of pure BY-CDs.

BY-CDs’ morphology was observed using a FEI Tecnai F20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, USA). BY-CDs’ optical properties were analyzed by a Hitachi F-2500 spectrofluorometer (Japan) and a PerkinElmer Lambda 35 ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (USA). Luminescence lifetimes of BY-CDs were measured by a Horiba Scientific QM8000 spectrofluorometer (Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of BY-CDs were carried out using a Thermo Fischer K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). BY-CDs’ fourier transform infrared (FTIR) data was acquired by a Thermo Fischer Nicolet iS5 FTIR spectrometer (USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of brilliant blue
ce:section.ce:para: To determine brilliant blue, a mixture was prepared by combining 250 μL BY-CDs solution (5 mg/mL) with varying volumes of brilliant blue. The resulting mixture was then diluted with PBS (pH = 7) to achieve a final volume of 3 mL. After incubation at ambient temperature for 5 min, the BY-CDs solution’s fluorescence spectrum was recorded using an excitation wavelength of 370 nm for quantitative analysis. The widths of the emission and excitation slits were adjusted to 10 nm. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F458/F558) of BY-CDs was used as the response signal. Different metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+ and Zn2+), vitamins (vitamin C and vitamin B1), amino acids (phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan and leucine), sugars (glucose, maltose, fructose and sucrose) and other potentially interfering substances (aspartame and acesulfame) were used for selectivity and anti-interference experiment, and the test process was similar to the aforementioned. The reliability of the proposed method was confirmed through analysis of spiked candy and beverage samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted visual detection of brilliant blue
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve portable and visual detection of brilliant blue, BY-CDs based fluorescence sensor was integrated with smartphone. Specifically, varying amounts of brilliant blue were added to the BY-CDs sensing system. The mixture solution was vigorously shaken to ensure uniformity. After incubation at ambient temperature for 5 min, the cuvette containing the mixed solution was subsequently moved to a UV light-equipped dark box emitting radiation at a wavelength of 365 nm. Fluorescence images were captured using the integrated camera of the smartphone (Vivo T1x), and then the color recognition software (Color Picker) installed on the smartphone was employed to identify and convert fluorescence color information into corresponding RGB values. The linear correlation between brilliant blue concentration and G/R value was ultimately established.

The test samples were chosen from a nearby supermarket, consisting of candy and beverages. The candy was first pulverized, and a precise measurement of 0.2 g pulverized candy was dissolved in 20 mL warm water. Subsequently, the candy sample solution was subjected to filtration using a membrane filter having a pore diameter of 0.22 μm to facilitate subsequent analysis. For beverage samples, the beverage was first ultrasonic treated for 1 h to eliminate CO2, followed by filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane for further analysis.

The prepared BY-CDs’ morphology features and size distribution were examined using TEM. As shown in Fig. S1A, the prepared BY-CDs have a near-spherical morphology and excellent dispersion. The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of BY-CDs shows a lattice fringe distance of 0.34 nm (the inset of Fig. S1A), which corresponds to the (0 0 2) diffraction facets of graphite-like multilayer structure [35]. This indicates that the synthesized BY-CDs have graphene-like structures. The size of BY-CDs particles varies between 1.9 and 4.7 nm, with an average particle size of 3.1 nm determined through statistical analysis of 100 BY-CDs particles (Fig. S1B).

The FTIR and XPS techniques were applied to examine the elemental composition and surface structure of the prepared BY-CDs. The BY-CDs’ FTIR spectra can be observed in Fig. S2. The wide absorption band representing the stretching vibration of OH/NH2 appears at 3425 cm−1 [36]. The stretching vibrations of CH can be attributed to the absorption bands observed at 2965 and 2925 cm−1 [37]. The absorption bands at 1695, 1631, 1225 and 1035 cm−1 can be explained by the CO/COOH, aromatic ring CC, CN and COH stretching vibrations, respectively [36,38]. The two characteristic absorption bands located at 1395 and 764 cm−1 were derived from CONH and benzene ring CH bending vibrations, respectively [39–41], indicating that acylated products of o-phenylenediamine and propyl gallate are attached to the surface of the BY-CDs. Meanwhile, XPS was measured to further analyze BY-CDs’ elemental composition. The XPS full-scan spectrum of BY-CDs (Fig. 1 A) shows that there are three characteristic peaks of C1s (285.08 eV), N1s (400.08 eV) and O1s (533.08 eV), and the corresponding element percentages are 67.8, 2.5 and 29.7 %, respectively. The BY-CDs’ internal structure and possible surface state were deeply investigated by high resolution XPS spectra. The C1s spectrum with high resolution (Fig. 1B) displays three distinct peaks at 284.44, 285.86 and 288.38 eV, belonging to CC, CN and OCO functional groups, respectively [36]. The N1s spectrum with high resolution (Fig. 1C) shows three distinct peaks at 398.55, 399.39 and 400.71 eV, assigning to CN, CN and NH functional groups, respectively [36,38]. The O1s spectrum with high resolution (Fig. 1D) displays two distinct peaks at 531.29 and 532.86 eV, attributed to CO and COH functional groups, respectively [36]. The findings from the FTIR and XPS analysis demonstrated a congruent outcome, suggesting that the synthesized BY-CDs predominantly consisted of C, O and N elements, and the surface had an abundance of nitrogen and oxygen function groups (e.g., OH, CONH, NH2). From the HRTEM, FTIR and XPS results, it can be speculated that the BY-CDs synthesized in this experiment are mainly composed of graphene-like carbon core center and surface molecular fluorophores (acylated products of o-phenylenediamine and propyl gallate). Fig. S3 shows the schematic structure of BY-CDs.

To investigate BY-CDs’ optical properties, the fluorescence and UV–vis spectra were performed. The absorption spectrum in the UV–vis range was illustrated in Fig. 2 A. The BY-CDs’ absorption spectrum in the UV–vis range shows two peaks. The π-π* transition of the CC group and the n-π* transition of the CO/CN group are reflected in absorption peaks at 207 and 273 nm, respectively [28,42]. At the maximum excitation wavelength of 410 nm, the corresponding maximum emission wavelengths is 558 nm. Under the irradiation of natural light and UV lamp (365 nm), the BY-CDs aqueous solution exhibits light yellow and bright yellow, respectively. The fluorescence spectrum was displayed in Fig. 2B. BY-CDs’ fluorescence spectrum exhibits two emission bands of blue and yellow at a single excitation wavelength. According to previous reports, the blue and yellow emission bands of BY-CDs are derived from carbon core and surface fluorophores, respectively [43,44]. From the fluorescence spectrum, it can be further discovered that the blue emission peak gradually shifts towards the longer wavelength when the excitation wavelength increases. Notably, the blue emission peak shows obvious excitation-dependent emission characteristics. On the contrary, the yellow emission peak exhibits excitation-independent emission characteristics, and its emission peak position remains basically unchanged at different excitation wavelengths. The excitation dependence of short wavelength emission and excitation independence of long wavelength emission are common phenomenon in dual-emission CDs [45–47]. Previous research pointed out that the excitation-dependent behavior of blue emission is resulted from internal carbon core with different particle sizes [48], while the excitation-independent behavior of yellow emission is related to the uniform emission state on the CDs surface [49]. Two emission peaks’ fluorescence intensity changes correspondingly with the increasing excitation wavelength. To enhance the sensitivity of detection and achieve the desired fluorescence intensity for both emission peaks, 370 nm was chosen as the most suitable excitation wavelength for further research. At the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the wavelengths of the two emission peaks are 458 and 558 nm, respectively. BY-CDs’ absolute fluorescence quantum yield determined by integrating sphere technique is 16 %.

In addition, the study examined the stability of BY-CDs when subjected to different experimental conditions. As depicted in Fig. S4A, BY-CDs’ fluorescence intensity at 458 and 558 nm remains basically unchanged when the NaCl concentration increases from 0 to 500 mM, suggesting that BY-CDs have good salt resistance. As depicted in Fig. S4B, BY-CDs’ fluorescence intensity at 458 and 558 nm remained unchanged after continuous excitation light irradiation for 120 min, suggesting that BY-CDs exhibit remarkable resistance to photobleaching. These results show that BY-CDs have exceptional fluorescence stability, which is crucial for their practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and ratiometric fluorescence detection of brilliant blue
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain a better response of BY-CDs to brilliant blue, the main parameters including the amount of BY-CDs solution, the pH of PBS and incubation time were tested. 50 μM of brilliant blue was used throughout the optimization experiment, and the response of BY-CDs to brilliant blue was assessed using F458/F558. The effect of BY-CDs amount on F458/F558 was studied. As illustrated in Fig. S5A, the F458/F558 value gradually increases with the increment in BY-CDs amount ranging from 100 to 250 μL. However, when the amount of BY-CDs exceeds 250 μL, the F458/F558 value remains basically unchanged. The appropriate amounts of BY-CDs selected in subsequent experiment was 250 μL. The influence of pH on F458/F558 was examined and depicted by Fig. S5B. The little change was observed in the value of F458/F558 as the pH value ranged from 4 to 10. Considering its simplicity and universal applicability, the subsequent experiment was carried out with the pH of PBS at 7. Additionally, the influence of incubation time on F458/F558 was researched. As displayed in Fig. S5C, the F458/F558 value increases with increasing incubation time and reaches stability within 5 min, indicating a rapid reaction between brilliant blue and BY-CDs, which is beneficial for rapid determination of brilliant blue. Therefore, the optimal incubation time was selected to be 5 min.

The sensing performance of BY-CDs towards brilliant blue was studied under the above optimal experimental conditions. Fig. 3 A shows the dual-emission fluorescence of BY-CDs when various concentrations of brilliant blue are introduced. With the rise in concentration of brilliant blue (0–120 μM), there was a gradual reduction observed in the intensity of the emission peak at 558 nm, however, no notable alteration was detected in the intensity of the emission peak at 458 nm. This fluorescence phenomenon provides a reference for internal calibration and eliminates the environmental interference in the determination of brilliant blue. Fig. 3B displays the relationship between F458/F558 and different concentrations of brilliant blue. There exists a strong linear correlation between the F458/F558 and brilliant blue concentration within the range of 0.01–120 μM. The equation for linear regression was F458/F558 = 0.01193[C] + 0.4754 (R2 = 0.9992), and the limit of detection (LOD) was established at 8.1 nM (S/N = 3). Moreover, we compared this method with previous reported methods for the determination of brilliant blue [6,50–53]. As displayed in Table S1, this developed method exhibits a wide detection range and high sensitivity. More importantly, the proposed dual-emission BY-CDs based sensor can eliminate the interference of various factors, and possesses the merits of straightforward operation, convenience, inexpensive and quick response, which is suitable for the effective determination of brilliant blue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of BY-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Good selectivity and anti-interference performance are essential for the developed sensor due to the complexity of the actual sample. Thus, the effects of different metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Na+ and Zn2+), vitamins (vitamin C and vitamin B1), amino acids (phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan and leucine), sugars (glucose, maltose, fructose and sucrose) and other potentially interfering substances (sodium benzoate, aspartame and acesulfame) on BY-CDs’ fluorescence intensity were researched. Fig. 4 A shows the changes of F458/F558 after adding 50 μM brilliant blue, different metal ions, vitamins, amino acids, sugars and other potentially interfering substances at a concentration of 500 μM. Obviously, only brilliant blue can effectively cause significant change in F458/F558, while other interfering substances have negligible effects on F458/F558. This indicates that BY-CDs exhibit favorable selectivity towards brilliant blue. Fig. 4B shows the changes of F458/F558 after adding different interfering substances to a BY-CDs solution containing 50 μM brilliant blue. In coexistence with these potential interfering substances, brilliant blue can still cause almost the same change on F458/F558, indicating that BY-CDs has excellent anti-interference against brilliant blue. These results suggest that BY-CDs possess the potential to serve as an outstandingly ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting brilliant blue in complex media.

To gain insights into the mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence quenching, relevant studies were conducted. Brilliant blue’s UV–vis absorption spectra and BY-CDs’ fluorescence spectra were measured and depicted by Fig. 5 A. The absorption peak of brilliant blue in the UV–vis spectrum is mainly between 500 700 nm, and there is a significant overlap with BY-CDs’ emission peak at 558 nm, suggesting that the occurrence of fluorescence quenching may potentially arise due to either inner filter effect (IFE) or FRET [54]. In general, FRET is associated with a shortening of BY-CDs’ fluorescence lifetime, whereas IFE does not induce any alterations in the BY-CDs’ fluorescence lifetime [55]. BY-CDs’ fluorescence lifetime was investigated to further elucidate the precise fluorescence quenching mechanism, both before and after the addition of brilliant blue. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, BY-CDs’ fluorescence lifetime before and after the introduction of brilliant blue was 1.89 ns and 1.43 ns, respectively. BY-CDs’ fluorescence lifetime becomes shorter after the addition of brilliant blue, which indicates that FRET is the main quenching mechanism [56]. The efficiency of energy transfer (E) was calculated using the equation: E = 1 − (τDA/τD), where τDA and τD are the fluorescence lifetime of BY-CDs in the presence and absence of brilliant blue, respectively. E is calculated to be 24.3 %. In addition, the absorption peak of brilliant blue in the UV–vis range mainly overlaps with BY-CDs’ emission peak at 558 nm, but does not overlap with the emission peak of 458 nm. Hence, the emission intensity at wavelength of 558 nm is more easily quenched, whereas the emission intensity at wavelength of 458 nm is basically unchanged. It is also beneficial for constructing ratiometric fluorescence sensors for the determination of brilliant blue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection performance of BY-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the practicability of BY-CDs based sensor for ratiometric fluorescence detection of brilliant blue in food samples, the standard addition method was applied to detect brilliant blue in candy and beverage samples. The recovery of brilliant blue ranges from 96.0 % to 104 %, as indicated in Table 1 , with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 2.52 %. These satisfactory results indicate that BY-CDs based fluorescence sensor has excellent ratiometric sensing properties, which can avoid the analyte-independent interferences, and exhibits great potential for practical implementation in monitoring brilliant blue in real food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BY-CDs based smartphone-assisted sensor for brilliant blue detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 A, the developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor presents a pronounced color change from yellow to green under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light when different concentrations of brilliant blue are added. This finding was additionally confirmed by employing the color coordinate diagram (Fig. 6B) provided by the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) and similar results were obtained. Therefore, a brilliant blue monitoring platform based on a smartphone was established, which utilized the color reading and analysis function of the smartphone for brilliant blue detection. The smartphone was used to capture the fluorescent image of the sensing system subsequent to the addition of varying concentrations of brilliant blue, and the smartphone software (Color Picker) identified and converted the color of the fluorescent image into R, G, and B values. As displayed in Fig. 6, the G/R exhibited a significant correlation with brilliant blue concentrations in the range of 0.01–100 μM. The equation for linear regression was G/R = 0.0165[C] + 0.9356 (R2 = 0.9947) and the LOD was 8.5 nM (S/N = 3). To evaluate the reproducibility of the BY-CDs based smartphone-assisted sensor for detecting brilliant blue, we performed five consecutive brilliant blue content analyses at concentrations of 10, 20, and 40 µM. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries were 102 %, 103 % and 98.1 % with corresponding RSD of 1.11 %, 2.02 % and 2.20 %, respectively. The results indicate that the developed BY-CDs based smartphone-assisted sensor has good reproducibility in brilliant blue detection. Subsequently, the brilliant blue in candy and beverage samples was determined to assess the practicality of the smartphone-assisted visual sensor. As shown in Table S3, the recovery ranges from 94.5 to 104 % with RSD < 4.2 %. The result indicates the constructed BY-CDs based smartphone-assisted visual sensor can be used as an efficient tool for real-time and visual detection of brilliant blue in real samples.

In this study, an innovative ratiometric fluorescence and smartphone-assisted visual sensor based on dual-emission BY-CDs was first established for detecting bright blue. The BY-CDs displayed dual fluorescence emission peaks at 458 and 558 nm when excited at 370 nm. Based on the change of F458/F558, the BY-CDs exhibits good ratiometric sensing performance for brilliant blue, with a detection range of 0.01–120 μM and an impressively low LOD of 8.1 nM. The BY-CDs based ratiometric fluorescence sensor shows fast response, good selectivity, strong anti-interference ability, excellent stability and heightened sensitivity. It was successfully used to detect brilliant blue in actual food samples. In addition, a BY-CDs based smartphone-assisted visual sensor was developed to visually detect brilliant blue. This work not only presents a novel approach to construct brilliant blue sensing platform with high accuracy, convenient and real-time, but also expands the utilization of CDs in the realm of ensuring food safety.

Lizhen Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Project administration. Meng Chen: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Ting Zhao: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Lin Yuan: Methodology. Zhi Mi: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Yunfeng Bai: Software, Methodology. Peng Fei: Software, Methodology. Zhixiong Liu: Software. Caiqing Li: Software, Investigation. Ligang Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Feng Feng: Project administration, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere appreciation for the assistance provided by the Higher Education Technology Innovation Projects of Shanxi Province (2021L375 and 2021L387).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124782.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
